summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Final parenting orders – Defended hearing – Where the children are aged six and seven years – Where it is uncontroversial the children should live with the mother and she should have sole decision-making authority for them – Dispute about the nature and extent of the children’s interaction with the father – Where the children’s time with the father has been supervised since late 2023 – Where the father committed family violence – Where the children were exposed to family violence – Where the father remains a risk of harm to the children and the mother – Where the supervised visits between the children and the father have been positive since August 2025 – Where the children love the father but remain cautious about him – Where the children do not want to see the father without someone else present – Where supervision by a family member or friend is not suitable – Where indefinite supervision is generally undesirable – Where the orders are final but not immutable – Orders made – Children to spend professionally supervised time with the father each alternative Sunday – Children to communicate electronically with the father.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

AUSTIN J:

These reasons explain the disposition of the parenting cause between the parties in respect of their children under Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). Their financial cause under Pt VIII of the Act is yet to be heard.

BACKGROUND

The parties began cohabitation in 2011, married in 2015, finally separated in late 2021, and divorced in late 2023.
The children were born in 2018 and 2019 and are now respectively seven and six years old.
Both parties live in NSW, though in different townships. The duration of the drive between their households is approximately 40 minutes.[1]
After the parties’ separation in late 2021, they arranged for the children to live with them for about equivalent time in rotations,[2] though the mother said she only agreed reluctantly.[3] That arrangement continued until January 2023, when the mother stopped the children from spending time with the father due to her belief he had assaulted the younger child.[4] For her, that was the final straw because, until then, she had struggled to suppress her misgivings about the risk of harm posed to the children by the father’s family violence, use of illicit drugs, and excessive use of alcohol.
The mother began these proceedings shortly afterwards in March 2023, seeking both parenting and financial relief. The father responded in June 2023.
Interim consent orders were made in May 2023 and July 2023 requiring the father to submit to hair-strand testing for his illicit drug use, the father to attend drug and alcohol counselling, and both parties to enrol in post-separation parenting courses. No orders were made at that time to regulate the children’s interaction with the father.
It was not until more interim consent orders were made in October 2023 that the children were required to spend professionally supervised time with the father for two hours each fortnight and required him to submit to more hair-strand drug testing. The children resumed seeing the father in November 2023,[5] not having seen him since February 2023.[6]
In April 2024, interim consent orders provided for the children to live with the mother and for them to spend supervised time with the father for three hours on the Saturday and Sunday of each alternate weekend, subject to his successful enrolment in a “Men’s Behaviour Change Program”. The orders relaxed the former supervision requirements by enabling the children’s time with the father to be supervised by the paternal grandmother or another family friend.[7]
The father enrolled in and eventually completed the designated program by December 2024,[8] so such orders prevailed until they were again amended in August 2025.
In August 2025, the interim orders were varied by reverting to the requirement for the children to only spend professionally supervised time with the father for three hours each alternate Sunday. The variation was sought by the mother and was eventually made with the father’s consent.[9] Again, he was ordered to attend upon counselling with a named counsellor and to submit to more hair-strand drug testing.
The upshot of the interim orders is that, since November 2023, the children have only ever spent supervised time with the father. Save for the period between April 2024 and August 2025 when the supervision was informally provided by a family member or friend,[10] the supervision has been provided by professional supervision agencies. The mother was discontent with the informal supervision by the paternal grandmother but is satisfied by the supervision provided by the professional agency under the interim orders most recently made in August 2025.[11]
On 16 December 2025, the paternal grandmother was consensually joined to the proceedings as the second respondent, but her joinder was only relevant to the pending financial cause under Pt VIII of the Act, yet to be tried. She was the father’s witness in the parenting cause, which was tried in February 2026.

PROPOSALS

The mother never deviated from her application for orders requiring the children to live with her and for her to have sole decision-making authority for them.
At the start of the trial, the mother abandoned the part of her application set out in her Amended Initiating Application filed on 20 November 2025 which proposed, in summary, that orders be made precluding the children from spending any time with the father and confining their interaction with him to intermittent electronic communication. Instead, she proposed the children spend professionally supervised time with the father for one day each couple of months.[12] However, by the time of final submissions, she adopted and endorsed the orders sought by the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”).
The ICL began the trial foreshadowing broad support for the mother’s position requiring the children’s future interaction with the father to be professionally supervised. The final minute of orders proposed by the ICL was tendered at the close of the evidence.[13] Essentially, she proposed the children spend professionally supervised time with the father for three hours each alternate Sunday and communicate with him electronically in intervening weeks.
The father initially sought the orders set out in his Further Amended Response filed on 26 November 2025 but, at the beginning of the second day of trial, tendered a Minute of Orders which slightly revised his proposal.[14] He agrees the children should live with the mother, who he concedes should also have sole decision-making authority for the children in respect of “long term” issues. However, he wants the children to spend time with him under a graduating regime which culminates two years hence in them spending time with him for five nights each fortnight in school terms and one-half of all school holidays. As a fall-back position, he wants the children to spend alternate Sundays with him. He disavowed the need for supervision.
As can be seen, the parties agreed the children should live with the mother and she should have sole decision-making authority for them. The controversy pertained to the nature and extent of the children’s interaction with the father.

EVIDENCE

The mother relied upon:
(a) her affidavit filed on 24 December 2025;

(b) the affidavit of Ms C filed on 23 December 2025; and

(c) the affidavit of Ms D filed on 23 December 2025.

The mother was denied leave to rely upon another witness due to the apparent irrelevance of her evidence.[15]
The father relied upon:
(a) his affidavit filed on 17 December 2025; and

(b) the affidavit of the paternal grandmother filed on 17 December 2025.

Notwithstanding r 8.15(3)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth), the annexures to the parties’ and witnesses’ affidavits were treated as forming part of the evidence.
The parties and the ICL relied upon:
(a) the Child Impact Report dated 23 June 2023; and

(b) the Family Report dated 27 November 2024.

Numerous exhibits were tendered.[16]

Legal principles

Orders in respect of children are made under Part VII of the Act, where the meaning of a “parenting order” is defined (s 64B). The Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper (s 65D), within the context of the objects of the legislation (s 60B).
When making parenting orders, the Court is mandated to regard the children’s best interests as the paramount consideration (s 60CA and s 65AA) and the Act specifies the criteria which must be considered when determining the form of orders which meets and promotes the children’s best interests (s 60CC).
Parental responsibility for children is vested in their parents (s 61C(1)), regardless of whether they live together or are separated (s 61C(2)), but that situation only applies whilst ever no order is made to change it (s 61C(3) and s 61D). Parental responsibility is defined to encompass all duties, powers, responsibilities and authority conferred by law upon parents (s 61B).
When parental responsibility for a child or children in relation to “major long-term issues” is retained by or allocated to more than one person, orders may prescribe whether those persons have joint or sole decision-making authority in relation to all or only specified issues (s 61D(3)). The Act defines “major long-term issues” to include those such as the child’s education, religion, culture, health, name, and changed living arrangements (s 4(1)) and specifies what “joint decision-making” requires of the parties with respect to decisions made about such issues (s 61DAA). One person allocated with parental responsibility for a child need not be consulted by another person allocated with parental responsibility in respect of minor decisions made for the child which fall outside “major long-term issues” (s 61DAB).

CHILDREN’S BEST INTERESTS

Sections 60CC(2)(b) and 60CC(3)

The mother and children identify as Aboriginal.[17] She capably inculcates the children with their cultural identity.[18]
The father said he was proud of the children’s indigenous heritage and realises the mother is best placed to foster their cultural identity.[19]

Sections 60CC(2)(a) and 60CC(2A)

The mother contended the orders made to regulate the children’s interaction with the father must be formulated to promote their safety by protecting them from family violence and abuse committed by the father. For her, this was the pivotal issue in the litigation.
In addition, the mother contends the orders ought not expose her to an unacceptable risk of harm from family violence committed by the father (s 60CG(1)(b)).

Family violence

The parties gave incompatible versions of the past violence in their relationship.
The mother’s evidence of the father’s violent, controlling and coercive conduct is accepted as being truthful and accurate. Cross-examination only served to enhance the reliability of her evidence. By comparison, the father’s evidence on the topic proved to be unreliable. He was impelled to concede on numerous occasions that his evidence was either false or misleading. While he was impelled to concede he had committed family violence in the past, his admission was still not as expansive as the facts found proven on the balance of probabilities.
The mother contended the father was both physically and emotionally abusive,[20] which contention was borne out by the evidence.
In 2012, the father head-butted the mother and called her a “black cunt”,[21] which tends to call into question his asserted sensitivity to and warm embrace of her aboriginality. His specific denial is rejected.[22]
In 2019, the father was angered and dragged the mother into the house by her hair.[23] His specific denial is rejected.[24]
In 2020, the father grabbed the mother by the arm and dragged her into the bedroom, where he abused her.[25]
In 2021, the father yelled abuse at the mother and threw objects around the house.[26]
On occasions when the father did not assault the mother, he intimidated her by his aggressive demeanour.[27] Other times he ignored her altogether.[28] She had to cater to his impulsive moods.[29]
The father controlled the mother’s social engagements and pried into her social media.[30] His specific denial is rejected.[31]
The father’s intimidation of the mother continued after they separated in late 2021.
In 2022, the father pressured the mother to help him defraud an insurer by claiming insurance to repair a car he damaged while driving it intoxicated.[32] The mother resisted his pressure but was intimidated nonetheless.
In April 2024, the father attended a public venue where he knew the mother and children would be present. Once there, he engaged with the children, even though he was then bound by the interim orders made in October 2023, which confined his interaction with them to two hours each fortnight under professional supervision.[33]
In May 2024, the father again attended a public venue where he knew the mother and children would be present. Once there, he again engaged with the children, even though he was then bound by the interim orders recently made in April 2024, which confined his interaction with them to six hours each fortnight under supervision.[34]
It is unnecessary to make any findings about whether the father intimidated the mother on those two occasions in April and May 2024, as she alleged but he denied. The father was found in parallel civil proceedings before a State court not to have intimidated the mother,[35] but the parties to and the witnesses in those State proceedings were different. Consequently, no issue estoppel was created by the finding of the State court (Blair v Curran [1939] HCA 23; (1939) 62 CLR 464 at 531; Jackson v Goldsmith [1950] HCA 22; (1950) 81 CLR 446 at 466; Re Wakim; Ex parte McNally [1999] HCA 27; (1999) 198 CLR 511 at [156]–[158]). Nor should the finding made by the State court on quite different evidence bind this Court, despite the existence of discretion to adopt the finding (s 102NN(3)(b). The father’s counsel accepted those propositions.
Regardless of the evidentiary conflict about the incidents in April 2024 and May 2024, the father was well aware of the terms of the interim parenting orders by which he was bound, yet he acted entirely inconsistently with their intended purpose of tightly constraining the children’s interaction with him. His contention that he did not think he was doing “anything wrong” on those two occasions is rejected.[36] He admitted in cross-examination that, looking back, he should not have done it and understood why the mother would have felt anxious.
In October 2024, the father drove a vehicle into the cul-de-sac in which the mother lived and then past her house for no apparent reason.[37] Inferentially, his motivation was to let her know he was watching her. That was a different incident from the similar incident months earlier in May 2024 alleged by the mother against the father,[38] which incident the State court rejected on the evidence adduced before it.[39] When cross-examined, the mother insisted there were two separate incidents. She said she reported both to police and separate incident reports were created by the police.
The father commonly called the mother derogatory and profane names.[40] The voice message he left her in February 2022 is the most vivid and disgraceful example.[41] It was played in Court. Aside from the deeply indecent language, his loud voice and hostile tone would have terrified the mother, as she described.[42] The father sounded as though he lost self-control. Such was the intimidatory nature of his message, the police issued a provisional family violence order against him for the mother’s protection, which was later converted into a final family violence, operable until August 2023.[43] The father admitted the voicemails he left the mother were “inappropriate”,[44] but that description fails to encapsulate such rank disrespect, contempt, and venom. Having heard the recording, the court child expert unremarkably said it would have made the mother anxious and hypervigilant.
The mother’s confidence was sapped by the way she was treated by the father. She was so stressed that her hair fell out in clumps, she felt ill, experienced intrusive thoughts, and was diagnosed with “post-traumatic stress” by the counsellor she consulted.[45] The court child expert observed the mother’s anxiety and hypervigilance in the proximity of the father.[46]
The father’s violent behaviour seems an aspect of his disposition and is not confined to domestic situations. In 2014, he assaulted a security guard at licensed premises, for which he was prosecuted, convicted and sentenced.[47] In 2015, he head-butted a woman outside licensed premises.[48] In 2022, he threatened reprisals against others.[49] In 2023, he assaulted and injured his new partner. In 2025, her stalked and intimidated his new partner. In respect of those offences committed against his new partner in 2023 and 2025, he was prosecuted, convicted, sentenced, and subjected to a family violence order for her protection.[50]
The children made multiple reports to the mother[51] and their counsellor[52] of them witnessing the father assaulting his new partner.
The father admitted the children were exposed to family violence,[53] due to which they are hypervigilant about the mother’s safety and apprehensive about their own safety. The mother’s honest belief the children have been “impacted emotionally and psychologically” by the father’s behaviour is undoubtedly true.[54] Their trepidation about the father has been witnessed by the person supervising their visits with the father,[55] and by their counsellor.[56]
The father deposed he was now enlightened about the insidious effects of family violence due to the courses he has undertaken and the counselling he has received,[57] which proposition was also advanced on his behalf in final submissions, but the evidence objectively demonstrates the father has not yet had such an epiphany.
To illustrate with a few examples:
(a) the father assaulted and injured his then partner in June 2023[58] and falsely told the family consultant in June 2023 there were “no unacceptable risk of harm concerns” within his household,[59] which misrepresentation he failed to ever correct after he was supposedly educated by the numerous post-separation parenting courses he completed shortly afterwards in July and August 2023.[60]

(b) the father attended a “Men’s Behaviour Change” program from May to December 2024 pursuant to interim orders made in April 2024,[61] yet:

(i) when the course started, he completed a domestic violence risk assessment form in which he falsely declared he had never coerced, threatened, intimidated, abused or isolated his partner or children;[62]

(ii) while the course was current, he told the course convenor the voicemail he left the mother in February 2022,[63] did not warrant a family violence order being made against him, as the convenor’s notes record him (inaccurately) saying:[64]

... Frustrated as [the father] says he had an AVO placed against him from a voicemail message he sent his ex partner in anger. [The father] stated that at the time he was angry though feels it didn’t warrant an AVO due to it being 12 months beforehand. The AVO was only for a month and was later thrown out ...
(iii) while the course was current, he intimidated the mother by driving down her street in October 2024;

(iv) while the course was current, he told the court child expert in consultations in October and November 2024 that the mother’s allegations against him were fabricated to influence the outcome of the proceedings;[65]

(v) only a month after the course finished, in January 2025, he filed an Amended Response proposing the children live with him instead of the mother, when he had never previously alleged she posed any risk of harm to the children; and

(vi) only a few months after the course finished, over a few weeks in May and June 2025, he stalked and intimidated his new partner.[66]

(c) the father commenced counselling in May 2025 and told the counsellor he was being unfairly victimised in the litigation conducted before this Court, as the counsellor’s notes record him saying:[67]

Guilty until proven innocent in family court. Vindictive partner can say anything to deny and delay seeing kids
(d) in the trial affidavit filed by the father in December 2025, he still falsely denied the mother’s allegations of his violent behaviour.
The evidence falls a long way short of demonstrating the father now has some genuine understanding of how his behaviour is destructive to others and threatens both the physical and emotional safety of the children and the mother. The evidence shows he remains a risk of harm to the children and the mother, which conclusion the court child expert independently reached.[68]
Given the acceptance of the mother’s evidence, this warning by the court child expert must be heeded:
The report writer is particularly concerned given [the mother’s] disclosure of several behaviours by [the father] that are associated with an increased risk of significant harm and lethality, including sexual coercion, threats of self-harm, and stalking post-separation.
The court child expert was concerned the children still perceived they and the mother were unsafe with the father, which state of heightened emotion can be psychologically harmful to them.[69] Their anxiety is reinforced by their intuition about the mother’s fear of the father,[70] though her reaction is inadvertent and not a deliberate ploy to influence the children.[71]
The only realistic antidote to the compounding anxieties of the mother and the children is to make orders which abate the fears of both the mother and the children. The mother and the ICL promulgated orders which the court child expert recommended.[72]

Abuse of children

In January and February 2023, the mother observed bruising on the body of the youngest child, which both children told her had been inflicted by the father hitting the youngest child.[73] The youngest child’s injuries were photographed.[74] The mother was sufficiently concerned by what she observed and what she was told that she reported the matter to police for investigation.
When challenged in cross-examination, the mother adhered to her evidence. She insisted the children gave her convincing details of the incidents, even to the point of the youngest child demonstrating a punching motion with a closed fist.
The children independently alleged to others the father had hit them, including their counsellor and the court child expert.[75]
The father denied responsibility for the youngest child’s injuries and alleged the mother was coaching the children to make false allegations against him,[76] though he ultimately conceded in cross-examination she acted reasonably and protectively by reporting the matter to police.
The father was not charged with any offence as the investigating police concluded the youngest child’s evidence was not sufficiently reliable,[77] though the court child expert said inconsistencies in the reports made by such young children did not necessarily mean their narrative was entirely false.[78] She gave evidence to similar effect during cross-examination.
The mother realised the evidence was not strong enough to support a positive factual finding the father had physically abused the children according to the civil standard of proof (s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth)), but she contended the evidence was strong enough to support an inference the father poses an unacceptable risk of harm to the children, which distinction is clear (Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 at [1]–[7] and [50]–[55]).
The mother’s submission is accepted. At the very least, the father has resorted to the physical discipline of the children, even from a very young age,[79] which physical correction is unlikely to be educative or instructive for such young children. The court child expert said the administration of physical discipline showed a lack of parenting capacity. In isolation, this evidence would probably not preclude the children from spending unsupervised time with the father but, in conjunction with the abundant evidence about his proclivity for violent outbursts with adults when he is angered, frustrated or irritated, the evidence corroborates the need for professional supervision.

Section 60CC(2)(b)

There is no doubt the children love the father. The family consultant observed the children run delightedly to him in June 2023.[80] They were just as eager to see him with the court child expert in October 2024.[81]
Despite the children’s love for the father, they remain cautious about him. They have told the mother they do not want to go to the father’s home or see him without someone else present.[82] The elder child has said much the same thing to the court child expert,[83] and the children’s counsellor.[84]
The children are both still young, so their views carry little weight, but it is noteworthy they remain anxious about extending their time with the father or seeing him in the absence of a supervising third party.

Section 60CC(2)(c)

The parties did not address any developmental, psychological, emotional or cultural needs of the children.

Section 60CC(2)(d)

The mother’s parenting capacity is not challenged. She meets their physical, emotional and intellectual needs satisfactorily.
The mother recognises the importance of the children’s relationships with the father and is only concerned with keeping them safe.[85] She admits the children’s supervised visits with the father since August 2025 have been positive experiences.[86] The father’s suspicions she is disinterested in promoting the children’s relationships with him is mistaken.[87]
The father’s illicit drug use would likely have been another barrier to the children’s unfettered interaction with him,[88] but the problem has now largely abated.[89] In May 2023, the father returned a hair-strand test which was positive for multiple illicit drugs and excessive alcohol use. Since then, his hair-strand and urine tests have been negative.[90]
All the same, much of the father’s offensive behaviour occurred when he was not intoxicated. His parenting capacity is undoubtedly impaired. He had no understanding of the children’s attachment needs when consulting the family consultant in June 2023.[91] He showed no insight into the children’s emotional needs at all when, in January 2025, he filed an Amended Response seeking the reversal of their residence. All that did was worry the children.[92]
The only insight the father displayed was by conceding he developed tunnel vision and lost sight of the children’s needs in the conflict.[93] Unfortunately, that was an isolated concession made in November 2024.

Section 60CC(2)(e)

Both children are receiving counselling, which the court child expert recommends should continue.[94] The youngest child consulted a speech pathologist,[95] and is being assessed for other conditions.[96]
The mother is attending adequately to those needs.

Section 60CC(2)(f)

No other features of the evidence were addressed.

CONCLUSION

It was agreed the children should live with the mother.
It was agreed the mother would have sole decision-making authority for the children in respect of “major long-term issues”, as defined in the Act (s 4(1)), though the corollary of such agreement is that both parties retain the parental responsibility for the children conferred on them by law (s 61C and s 61D). The order gives the mother the power of veto over decisions concerning important issues in the children’s lives.
It was agreed the children should spend time with the father. The vexation concerned the frequency, duration, and professional supervision of the visits.
The father submitted the perpetuation of supervised visits would tend to entrench the children’s anxiety about him, which potentiality cannot be discounted. The court child expert agreed with the proposition but emphasised it was only a potentiality. She said in cross-examination the continued professional supervision of the children’s visits with the father could either be an advantage or disadvantage, depending upon the father’s behaviour during the visits. Ultimately, she endorsed the recommendation she made in the family report, for the children to spend professionally supervised time with the father for a few hours each alternate weekend.[97] Such opinion evidence is accepted.
The Court should aspire to the objective of children deriving benefit from healthy relationships with both parents, but not at the expense of their safety (s 60CC(2)(a) and s 60CC(2)(e)). Professional supervision is required here for the foreseeable future to ensure the children are protected from the risk of physical and psychological harm posed by the father. An order for supervision will have the ancillary benefit of protecting the mother from subjection to family violence (s 60CG(1)).
While the father would prefer the children spend time with him without the need for supervision, he accepted his application may fail. In that event, he said he would prefer to see the children more frequently for shorter periods than infrequently for longer periods, which desire matches both the proposal eventually jointly made by the mother and the ICL and the recommendation of the court child expert. The orders will provide for the children to spend professionally supervised time with the father for three hours each alternate Sunday.
The father also said he was willing to pay for the supervision, which should be the case when he conceded he had fallen into arrears with child support payments. Even if he does rectify the arrears, as he said he is now doing, it shows he is an unreliable child support payer and the mother may have to bear the primary financial burden of maintaining the children. She can hardly afford to share the cost of supervision.
The ICL confirmed in submissions, without objection from either party, that the current professional supervisor could service the orders proposed by the ICL and the mother.
The children’s professionally supervised visits with the father have been a positive experience for them,[98] so perpetuating such orders for the short to mid-term is ideal. However, it was properly recognised that final orders requiring indefinite supervision, which in this case has the potential to last for 12 years, is generally undesirable (Slater & Light [2013] FamCAFC 4; (2013) 48 Fam LR 573 at 583–584; Champness & Hansen [2009] FamCAFC 96; (2009) FLC 93-407 at [209]–[215]; Moose v Moose (2008) FLC 93-375 at [119]).
Submissions therefore turned to the question of whether the orders should either provide for indefinite supervision, leaving it to the parties to bring fresh proceedings seeking a variation of the orders if and when they see fit (s 65DAAA), or a finite period of supervision, upon the expiration of which the requirement for supervision would be discharged.
As a third option, it is open to the Court to impose a finite period of supervision followed by a review (Slater & Light at 591; Champness & Hanson at [219]–[225]; W v W (Abuse allegations: unacceptable risk) [2005] FamCA 892; (2005) FLC 93-235 at [164]), but that course would necessitate the orders being only interlocutory in nature and the litigation would remain live. Neither the parties nor the ICL contemplated that as a viable option when the necessary period of supervision was contemplated to be measured in years rather than months.
In an exercise of discretion, the orders requiring supervision are made on a final basis and are indefinite. While such orders are final, they are not immutable. If the father can demonstrate a material change in his circumstances at some point in the future, he can petition the Court for variation of the orders (s 65D(2) and s 65DAAA). It may be that, in due course, the mother observes such a material change in the father’s demeanour that satisfies her it would be safe to voluntarily dispense with the need for supervision. The orders of the Court only prevail whilst ever the parties cannot reach their own agreement.
To alternatively make final orders which impose professional supervision for a finite period would entail an arbitrary guess about the desirable length of the supervision period. Judgments ought not be mere guesses. The mother acknowledged in cross-examination that final orders for the professional supervision of the children’s interaction with the father once they approach adolescence may be less than ideal, but she emphasised her current concerns about the father and sensibly said she could not see that far into the future. Her evidence was sound. Nobody can be clairvoyant about either the father’s capacity to improve or the fulfilment of his potential to do so.
The children’s supervised visits with the father are suspended during the first half of all school holiday periods, so the mother will have a clear period within which to take the children away on vacations, including international vacations. Her proposal in that regard was not the subject of any articulated objection. The father expressly agreed to the orders enabling the mother to take the children overseas.
The orders require the parties to ensure the children communicate electronically with the father at 5.00 pm on the Sundays when they do not spend professionally supervised time with him, on their birthdays, on Father’s Day, and on Christmas Day. That order was not controversial. No order is made requiring the parties to additionally facilitate telephone calls requested by the children because it is aspirational and unenforceable.[99]
The father is restrained from engaging with the mother or the children other than in the confined way prescribed by the orders. The injunction is not made so expansively as the mother and the ICL proposed.[100] In some respects, their proposal lacked sufficient prescription and was aspirational. The father’s contrary proposal for an order enabling him to attend school functions and sporting fixtures is rejected.[101]
Both parties are restrained from administering physical discipline to the children and from exposing the children to denigration of either parent.
The remaining orders were not and could not have been the subject of any reasonable resistance.
Some orders proposed by the parties and the ICL were unnecessary because the parties retain parental responsibility for the children.[102]
No order is made compelling the parties to communicate via a specified electronic platform.[103] They may do so if they wish.
Such orders are made in the best interests of the children.
